column1: # Function-style defq: bind a name to a code body, then invoke.
# `defq NAME [body]` registers NAME → list-body in the defq stack.
# Subsequent occurrences of NAME splice the body's elements at the
# pointer so they execute inline against the current stack.
# Spec subset semantics: lists bound via `defq` are always treated as
# code bodies — there's no Quoted/Eval distinction here. The full
# Go engine uses an Eval flag and a `quoteq` word to discriminate
# data lists from code bodies; both are listed in the engine.ts
# PARITY GAP and would need separate ports to support `defq x [data]`
# as data.
# Test word:
#   defq — sig [Word, Any]. Captures the name from a Word token,
#         binds it to the value in the defq stack. Returns [].
# tokens
defq double [ dupq addq ] 5 double
column3: body executes against stack=[5]: dupq→[5,5], addq→[10]

column1: defq square [ dupq mulq ] 7 square
defq inc [ 1 addq ] 5 inc
column3: body adds 1 to top of stack

column1: defq add5 [ addq 5 ] 10 add5
column3: curried form — body uses an embedded constant

column1: defq double [ dupq addq ] ( 3 double ) ( 5 double )
column3: parens isolate each invocation; without them the inner addq would greedily grab the second arg

column1: defq double [ dupq addq ] defq quad [ double double ] 3 quad
defq pi 3 pi addq 1
column3: bare scalar defq still substitutes (existing simple-value path)
